name: Harut
description: Harut is an angel mentioned in Islamic tradition, specifically in the Quran (2:102). He is often associated with Marut, forming a pair of angels whose story revolves around a divine test of humanity. According to some interpretations, these angels, critical of human fallibility, were challenged by God to demonstrate their own righteousness when subjected to earthly temptations. They were subsequently sent to Earth and succumbed to human desires, including intoxication and illicit relationships, thereby proving humanity's inherent weakness and the necessity of divine guidance. Their story serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of hubris and the seductive power of temptation. The angels are often associated with teaching humans magic, although the nature and purpose of this magic is subject to varied interpretations, ranging from legitimate knowledge to harmful sorcery. Their narrative highlights complex theological themes concerning free will, divine judgment, and the inherent flaws of both angelic and human beings.
subtitle: The Fallen Angel

keyMyths.title: The Descent to Earth
keyMyths.description: Harut and Marut, critical of human behavior, were challenged by God to live as humans and resist temptation. They descended to Earth, but quickly succumbed to earthly desires. They fell in love with a beautiful woman named Zohra (often identified with Venus), who demanded they reveal the secret to ascending back to heaven. This act of revealing divine secrets cemented their fall and resulted in their punishment.

keyMyths.title: The Teaching of Magic
keyMyths.description: While on Earth, Harut and Marut are said to have taught humans magic. Some interpretations suggest this was done to test humanity, distinguishing between good and evil. Others portray it as a consequence of their fall, providing humans with knowledge that could be used for harmful purposes. This teaching is often linked to the separation of a man and his wife, a common element in stories of black magic.
keyMyths.source: Ibn Kathir, *Tafsir al-Qur'an al-'Azim* (Commentary on the Great Quran)

keyMyths.title: The Punishment
keyMyths.description: As punishment for their transgressions, Harut and Marut were given a choice: endure worldly torment or eternal damnation. They chose earthly suffering and are said to be suspended upside down in a well in Babylon, where they remain as a warning to others. They are chained and will remain so until the Day of Judgment, a constant reminder of the consequences of disobedience to God's will.

sources.author: Wheeler, Brannon M.
sources.work: *Prophets in the Quran: An Introduction to the Quran for Western Readers*
sources.description: Provides context for understanding Quranic narratives, including the story of Harut and Marut.
sources.text: Provides context for understanding Quranic narratives, including the story of Harut and Marut.
